summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews Carole Marshall's memoir about teaching in urban Providence schools, using it to critique how standardized testing policies have driven talented teachers out of education. Marshall developed effective, relevant curriculum that engaged poverty-stricken students, but quit after her school system was taken over by rigid testing mandates that forced canned curriculum and eliminated creative teaching approaches. O'Neil argues that while reform rhetoric focuses on removing 'bad teachers,' these policies simultaneously push out dedicated, inspirational educators. The post connects individual teacher experience to the broader national disaster of testing-obsessed education reform.
key_points: Standardized testing policies force teachers to abandon effective, student-relevant curriculum for expensive canned materials from publishers who 'knew nothing about urban teaching'
Administrative surveillance (with 'shiny new iPads') enforces rigid conformity, requiring all teachers to be on the same page every day regardless of student needs
The focus on removing 'bad teachers' ignores the collateral damage of losing talented, dedicated educators who refuse to participate in what they see as non-teaching
Education reform has created perverse incentives including consultant scams and cherry-picking students to game arbitrary metrics
The correlation between poverty and low test scores becomes obvious when you understand students' actual struggles, not just their numbers
money_quotes: After spending years refining strategies for getting my students to become enthusiastic readers and writers on thoughtful, relevant curriculum, I was being forced to teach canned curriculum purchased for millions of dollars from textbook publishers who knew nothing about urban teaching.
Every path to good teaching was effectively blocked off.
What was going on in the classrooms could no longer be called teaching.
She makes this shit very very real.
We are so used to pointing at examples of bad and defeated teachers and saying that they are the problem.
It is painful to imagine how many great teachers have left the educational system because of No Child Left Behind and Race To The Top.
key_insight: Education reform policies designed to weed out bad teachers are simultaneously driving out the most dedicated and effective ones, and we're not even measuring that loss.
